replies.ex-01-ruy-lopez-m5: Looking at this Ruy Lopez position, the standard plan is to gain space on the queenside and question the bishop. {"move": "b7b5"}
replies.ex-02-sicilian-najdorf-m6: The main line here is the English Attack setup. {"move": "Be3"} develops with ideas of f3 and Qd2.
replies.ex-03-french-advance-m4: Black should strike at the center immediately before White consolidates the pawn chain. {"move": "e6e5"}
replies.ex-04-caro-kann-m5:
Develop the kingside knight first; the bishop on f5 is already well placed.

replies.ex-05-qgd-m5: I would castle kingside here. It is the safest continuation and connects the rooks before Black commits the queenside pawns.
replies.ex-07-london-m4: Castling short keeps the king safe before the center opens. {"move": "e8g8"}
replies.ex-08-english-m4: Recapturing the pawn is consistent; the knight lands on a strong central square. {"move": "f6d5"}
replies.ex-09-scholars-defense-m3: The queen raid must be blunted right away, and g6 hits the queen while covering the mate threat on f7. {"move": "g7g6"}
replies.ex-11-vienna-m4: Taking on e5 opens the f-file for the rook, which is the whole point of the Vienna setup. {"move": "f4e5"}

note: Authored, reviewed replies representing typical base-checkpoint and adapted-checkpoint behavior on the held-out suite. Replayed runs mark every attempt reply_source='replayed', carry no provider request ids, and never pose as live model attempts. The base replies deliberately mix clean JSON, prose, SAN inside JSON, an illegal move, and one truncated reply; the adapted replies are uniform bare JSON, including answering the repeated position identically where the base run sampled two different answers.
